items.fix: Write the base case first, *then* the recursive case.
items.why: Without a base case the function calls itself forever and raises `RecursionError`.

items.mistake: Recursive case that doesn't make strict progress toward the base case.
items.fix: Ensure each recursive call uses a strictly smaller / closer-to-base argument.
items.why: If the argument never shrinks (or shrinks intermittently), recursion never terminates.
items.example:
```python
# Wrong: passes the same n on the recursive path:
def bad(n):
    if n == 0: return 1
    return bad(n)   # never reaches 0
# Right: bad(n - 1)
```

items.mistake: Naïve recursion on overlapping sub-problems (e.g. Fibonacci).
items.why: Repeated work explodes to O(2ⁿ); memoization collapses it to O(n).

items.mistake: Recursing into a 100k-node structure in Python.
items.fix: Rewrite as an iterative loop with an explicit stack.
items.why: Python's default recursion limit is ~1000; deep recursion raises `RecursionError`.
items.example:
```python
def walk(root):
    stack = [root]
    while stack:
        n = stack.pop()
        if n is None: continue
        process(n)
        stack.extend(reversed(n.children))
```

items.mistake: Mutating a shared default argument across recursive calls.
items.fix: Use `None` as the default and create a fresh container inside the function.
items.why: `def f(seen=[])` shares the same list across calls — values leak between invocations.
